content.text: The SaaS companies driving the most
demos and sign-ups from AI search and
SEO aren't doing anything complex at
all. In fact, the strategy they're
following is embarrassingly simple.
They're actually just working out what
their dream clients are searching on
Google organic LLMs when they actually
have high sales intent for their
software or solutions. This is a
playbook we run actively for 45 plus
SaaS companies like Orbit B, Fibler,
Proposify, and tons more. So, in this
video, I'm going to break down exactly
how you can audit where you stand today
in LLMs and Google organic. The full
blueprint so you can start ranking and
getting recommended for those dream
clients needing your software right now
and an exact play-by-play so you can
just copy it. So, with that said, let's
dive in.
So, let's dive into what you actually
need to do to nail down your SaaS SEO
and AI search strategy to start driving
pipeline and revenue. So, first off, you
need to work out what your money
keywords and money prompts are. These
are what your dream clients are going to
search for, not when they're messing
around information gathering, but when
they actually have buying intent for
your software or solutions. So, what I
recommend is building out what I call a
money keyword matrix. It usually has
four main tabs. If you're founder of the
company, perhaps you can work on this
yourself or you may want to bring in
some folks from your marketing, support,
sales, and CS teams. What you're going
to do is break down these four columns.
So, you can fire up a Google Sheet,
build out four columns. Column one is
what's every way someone can refer to
our software or solution. Column two is
what are the main industries that we
serve best. Column three is main
competitors in the market. And column
four is what are our dream clients' jobs
to be done or struggling moments. And
the reason I say you want to bring in
your support team, your sales team, your
CS team is cuz they can help with all
these insights or if you already have
all this customer research as the
founder, then you may know all these
golden nuggets. So, column one is what's
every way someone can refer to our
solution. So, one of our clients,
Fibler, in the LinkedIn ads attribution
space. In that case, it could be things
like LinkedIn ads attribution software,
tools, platforms, or likewise for
Proposify, our proposal client, it could
be proposal software, proposal tools,
proposal platforms, quoting software,
quoting platforms, you get the gist.
What's every different nuance way that
someone can refer to your main solution?
That's column one. Nail down all those.
Column two is what are all the
industries that we serve best? It's not
just industries we like to serve, but if
we look in our CRM, what are the
industries we've historically sold well
into? They have the problem we solve,
they're motivated to solve it, they have
cash to easily invest in our solution.
So, it could be healthcare, it could be
fintech, be sports, it could be sales,
it could be HR, finance, the list goes
on. Review your CRM, get your sales team
and kind of really understand what we
call these money niches. Number three,
super important for SaaS especially, is
what are the main competitors in the
field? What are the main competitors
that almost come up to an annoying
level? They're always brought up on
sales calls or we're always compared
against them. So, in Fibler's space, as
LinkedIn Ads Attribution, that could be
HockeyStack, that could be Dreamdata,
that could be Visible, and a bunch of
others. In ProposalFire's space, in the
proposal software space, that could be
companies like GetAccept, it could be
PandaDoc, or it could be a bunch of
others. And then, the fourth and final
column in this exercise is what are our
dream clients' jobs to be done, their
struggling moments, the main pain points
they're looking to try and fix. So, in
the proposal world, ProposalFire, could
be how to craft sales proposals that
convert. In the LinkedIn Ads Attribution
space, it could be how to justify the
ROI of LinkedIn Ads, or how to work out
how much revenue my LinkedIn Ads are
producing, those kind of things. Really
good exercise to look back at your sales
calls. So, if you record calls with a
tool like Fathom or similar, you can
kind of look at those juicy problems and
questions that keep coming up in sales
calls and then look to address those.
And then, once you've got those four
columns, the whole process here is to
make what we call long-tail money
keywords, or also longer-tail money
prompts. We want to leverage these
columns, these keywords together to work
out what our dream clients are actually
going to search for when they need our
software, ideally as soon as possible.
So, what you're probably thinking is
things like, "Oh, Sam, if I want to rank
for proposal software, ProposalFire's
space, that's going to be super
competitive." You'd be right, that's
probably a longer-term kind of 6 to
12-plus month exercise. It's going to
take time to build the content and
backlinks, build your authority, get
brand mentions, and much, much more to
eventually secure that ranking placement
on Google, and also get the brand
mention on LLMs, start driving inbounds.
But, as we always say, there's riches in
the niches, so making these longer-tail
money keywords or prompts is why we've
done these other tables to our money
keyword matrix. So, we want to add on
our industry. So, in the proposal
software space, we'd do money keyword
plus industry. So, it could be things
like best proposal software for fintech,
for healthtech, for sales teams, for HR,
and beyond. And in our LinkedIn ads
attribution space, we could be LinkedIn
ads attribution for SaaS, fintech,
healthtech, and so forth. And then, if

good for SaaS. So, oftentimes, we know
that folks will try a tool, try a

frustrated with their incumbent
provider, their incumbent software,
because pricing goes too high, maybe
customer support drops off, gets worse,
or maybe seats, costings, or support
just goes sky-high or similar. So, what
folks will search for is things like
competitor alternative. So, they might
search things like HockeyStack
alternative or Visible alternative or
Dreamdata alternative. And in Fibbl's
case, for our client, or they might
search for a comparison like Fibbl
versus HockeyStack or Dreamdata versus
HockeyStack. Or in Proposify's case, the
proposal software, they might search
Proposify versus GetAccept. Or they
might search PandaDoc versus GetAccept.
And these are all kind of real money
terms, where you can attract those dream
clients that are not messing around, are
actively searching to move from their
current software or weigh up current
options. And that's where you can
highlight where your product shines
through, where it's a great fit, and
start attracting dream clients who are
in market. And then, the last column,
the jobs to be done, the struggling
moments, the crisp and specific pain
points ICP or dream clients is where you
can kind of leverage those folks that
are earlier in the funnel, more on the
information stage, but kind of have that
specific pain points so you can leverage
your product as that pain killer for the
specific issues, pain points, and more
that it solves. And what you'll find is
a lot of these long-tail money niche
keywords and the longer-tail prompts
will have low search volume. If you look
them up on tools like Ahrefs, especially
things like competitor alternatives or
competitor one versus competitor two or
competitor pricing or competitor
reviews, it's often very low search
volume. But, the good thing is they have
low keyword difficulty, which means you
can build out these pages with the right
structure format content technical
and more. And they can often rank super
quick. We've seen competitor pages rank
as quick as days. So, for Fibler, for
example, we rank for things like
HockeyStack alternatives or Dreamdata
alternatives and a bunch of others. For
our other client, AB to B, in the
visitor identification space, we're
ranking for things like Leadfeeder

high-intent prospects that are not
messing around, but actively evaluating
solution or looking to move away from
their current vendor. And that's where
you can get quick sign-ups, quick leads,
and quick demos for your software. And
like I say, super low keyword
difficulty. So, with the right strategy
and content, you can rank super fast and
also get mentioned and recommended in
LLMs and AI search. And by the way, if

specific problem that our software

more simple. And you can use tools like

process.
But, if you want to do it the old school

either one, Google your money keyword.
So, in this case, if we were looking at

Are they in the top? And then on a tool

recommended when you do that kind of
money prompt search? If we're not
recommended in ChatGPT, then we need to

of pages that are actually being pulled
from. What you'll usually find for these
kind of money bottom of funnel terms,
it's usually listical based articles.
So, in this case, an article which would
be much like we reviewed the best hockey
stack alternatives of 2026 or we
reviewed the best Leadfeeder
alternatives of 2026. And this tends to
be the same for both ChatGPT and Google

lists, round up listicals, along with
the usual suspects like G2, Capterra,
maybe a few Reddit sections are usually
what you see pulled into these results.
So, working out are you ranking on
Google organic? Are you being pulled
into ChatGPT, Claude, other LLMs, and
being actively recommended? If not, what
type of content is being pulled in?
Let's look at that. Then, most
importantly, let's look to control the
narrative and build that piece of
content for this money keyword or prompt
firstly on our own website before we
worry about off-page SEO. So,
understanding what type of page is
ranking already, in this case if it's an
X alternative term, it's probably a
listical, best X alternatives 2026. And
what I recommend you do is follow a
blowout author strategy. So, at a high
level building these pages is giving a
quick summary up front of what the
article is about, do a quick comparison
table of who the options are. Typically,
you position yourself number one on the
list, but back it by customer research.
Here's where we're a great fit, here's
where we're a poor fit, here's why
customers choose us, here's a review of

here's a screenshot or a GIF of our

the nine other competitors. Actually
reviewing their website, reviewing their
tool, reviewing their software, taking
reviews and ratings from G2 and similar,
and doing a fair non-biased comparison
of all the other tools on the market.
And yeah, you can go through that for
all of your money keywords, working out
do you have pages ranking on Google
organic already? Perhaps they're lagging
at the bottom of page one or on page two
on Google, so maybe they need an

maybe need to add some more social
proof, maybe want to add some unique
stats, maybe you want to add an FAQ

you want to drive some backlinks to it
or get some brand mentions from external
niche relevant websites. Or likewise, if
you're not being recommended in ChatGPT,
then first like I say, you really want
to control the narrative, build each
money keyword into a dedicated page,
whether that's an article listicle,
whether that's a how-to article, whether
that's a landing page, a vertical page,
an ICP page, whatever makes sense,
whatever intent hits right for that

which is called off-page SEO and a bit
of mix of digital PR. So, reaching out
to folks, maybe you see that other

consistently mentioned for some of your

for an exchange, maybe you add them to

might be able to do some kind of
exchange, like if they give you a

you can feature them on your podcast or
do some kind of interview with them or
some kind of pro bono or fair exchange.
And the last part of the strategy is to
stay focused and avoid short-term hacks.
What do I mean by short-term hacks?
Well, a bunch of SaaS companies, some
big SaaS companies have been hit
recently because they've tried to churn
out a ton of articles, pages, and
similar on their website with a hacky
strategy. They've used AI to completely
generate the content. And what's
happened is they've seen a quick win, so
they've seen a surge in organic traffic,
but then Google has detected that the
content is low-value, spammy. And a
while back, Google rolled out a helpful
content update, and this traffic
suddenly dipped like crazy cuz they
realized this content was low-value,
spammy, and similar. And it's not to say
that AI content is terrible, cuz you can
definitely use AI to pull customer
research, pull insights, give a solid
outline for a page, but then I always
recommend a human touch so you can make
sure your page not just ranks, but
resonates and appeals to your dream
clients, especially if you're selling a
high contract value solution. You don't
want terrible content where someone's
going to land on the page or see a bit
of it cited in AI search and think these
folks don't have a clue about my world,
my problems, my goals, my motivations,
and so forth. So, staying focused with
the strategy, it's all about basically
following through everything talked
about, working out exhaustive list of
your money keywords, your money prompts,
reviewing every single money keyword and
money prompt on both Google organic AI
searches, LLMs, working out what types

that full moat on your website, so
working out what type of pages you need
to build for each money keyword and
money prompt. Is it a landing page? Is
it an industry page? Is it a comparison
article? Is it a comparison landing
page? Is it a best of list? And so

useful, and positioning your product as
the solution and painkiller. Once you've
nailed everything on your own website,
then it's about doing the off-page side

that are being mentioned in AI search
LLMs, as well as ones that are ranking

or offering them something in return, or
some of those will be paid to play. But
also getting featured on relevant sites
that are being pulled up a lot in LLMs,
so you'll find review sites like G2,
Capterra, and similar usual suspects.
Maybe getting active on Reddit threads
if you've got the resources. And
likewise, YouTube if you've got the

if you stick at this strategy, many,
